title: Analytical Framework for Fast Identification of Hydrogeological Boundaries and Aquifer Parameters in Confined Aquifers

research.question: Can pumping-test drawdown identify boundary type, image-well position, linear boundary geometry, transmissivity, and storage coefficient in one objective workflow?
research.system: A constant-rate well and at least three observation wells in a homogeneous isotropic confined aquifer with either no boundary, a linear recharge boundary, or a linear impervious boundary.
research.inputData: Time-drawdown records from Tongue Valley, Montana, and Dry Lake, Nevada, plus literature slope-ratio cases used to construct Bayesian likelihoods.
research.method: Segment semi-log drawdown curves, classify boundary type from the late-to-early slope ratio, select the corresponding image-well superposition, optimize image-well coordinates and aquifer parameters by simulated annealing, and construct the perpendicular-bisector boundary.
research.benchmark: Field results are compared with the documented recharge boundary at Tongue Valley and impervious boundary at Dry Lake; three-well solutions are compared with four-well solutions using SEE, ME, and MAE.
research.uncertainty: Bayesian posterior probabilities account for overlap in empirical slope-ratio classes, and well-combination error indices test solution stability. The framework does not resolve unconfined, leaky, or heterogeneous responses that depart from Cooper-Jacob straight-line behavior.
research.conclusion: The workflow recovered the documented boundary types and stable boundary and aquifer estimates at both field sites; at least three observation wells are recommended.

narrative.throughline: Read the boundary's signature from a change in semi-log slope, convert the signature into the correct image well, and turn the image geometry into a mapped hydrogeological boundary.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A pumping-test curve bends when a recharge source or flow barrier begins to influence drawdown.
narrative.stakes: Conventional graphical constructions require a boundary type in advance and can fail when observation-well circles do not share one intersection.
narrative.resolution: A Bayesian slope classifier and global inverse search replace subjective drawing, after which a perpendicular bisector returns the inferred linear boundary to map space.

background.visibleEvidence: Three distance circles nearly intersect but leave a triangular gap instead of one image-well point.
background.whyNow: The ambiguity propagates into boundary location and aquifer parameters.
background.boundary: The paper replaces this graphical step for linear boundaries in confined aquifers.

background.stepDetail: Grow three circles from observation wells and stop them at their incompatible intersections.
background.transitionBridge: Use the drawdown curve itself to determine the boundary type before locating the image well.

background.premise: In the two field cases, early- and late-time slopes support classification of recharge and impervious boundaries.
background.visibleEvidence: Each field drawdown trace separates into early- and late-time slopes before resolving to a recharge or impervious label.
background.whyNow: The late-to-early slope ratio can serve as a boundary-type observable.
background.boundary: This premise is limited to the documented Tongue Valley and Dry Lake applications.
background.locator: PDF page 10, Conclusions, field-case classification summary

background.visualObject: Early- and late-time slope pairs from the two field cases resolving to recharge and impervious labels
background.motionPurpose: Connect the two documented slope pairs to their source-reported boundary classes.
background.stepDetail: Split each field trace into early and late slopes; carry the pair to the classifier; reveal only the reported recharge and impervious outcomes.
background.transitionBridge: Measure the two slopes objectively with a breakpoint fit.

background.premise: Image-well theory converts a linear bounded aquifer into an infinite-domain superposition problem.
background.visibleEvidence: A recharge image injects across the boundary, whereas an impervious image pumps symmetrically.
background.whyNow: Once boundary type is known, the sign of the image well defines the forward model used in inversion.
background.boundary: The construction assumes a straight boundary and homogeneous isotropic radial flow.
background.sourceExcerpt: aquifer with a linear boundary to be transformed into the one of infinite areal extent.
background.visualObject: pumping well mirrored across a straight boundary
background.motionPurpose: Translate boundary behavior into a source or sink outside the physical aquifer.
background.stepDetail: Reflect the pumping well across the boundary and switch the image arrow sign by boundary class.
background.transitionBridge: Infer that image's coordinates from all observation-well drawdowns.

methods.input: Observed drawdown plotted against logarithmic pumping time at each well.
methods.output: An optimized breakpoint separating early-time and late-time drawdown segments.
methods.validity: The selected breakpoint minimizes the total fitting error for the two-segment representation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the breakpoint location by solving a constrained least squares regres-
methods.visualObject: semi-log drawdown curve with a movable breakpoint and two fitted lines
methods.motionPurpose: Replace subjective line drawing with an optimized split.
methods.stepDetail: Slide one breakpoint until the total two-segment residual reaches its minimum.
methods.transitionBridge: The fixed split exposes one segment on each side for regression.

methods.input: Early-time and late-time drawdown segments separated by the optimized breakpoint.
methods.output: One fitted linear regression for each separated drawdown segment.
methods.validity: Each segment is represented by a linear regression on the semi-logarithmic drawdown plot.
methods.sourceExcerpt: segments. Linear regression is then applied to each segment, producing
methods.visualObject: two regression lines locked to the separated drawdown segments
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the optimized temporal split into two fitted regression lines.
methods.stepDetail: Draw one linear regression through each fixed segment and retain the two fitted lines.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted slopes become the two inputs for the explicit ratio calculation in M03.

methods.input: Early-time slope m_1 and late-time slope m_2 for one drawdown record.
methods.operation: Use slope ratio as diagnostic metric
methods.output: Slope-ratio metric for identifying boundary type.
methods.validity: Section 2.3 states that the slope ratio serves as the boundary-type metric.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Slope ratio estimation, paragraph following Figure 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: serves as a metric in identifying the boundary type.
methods.visualObject: The fitted slope pair collapsing into one diagnostic ratio marker
methods.motionPurpose: Show the source-stated ratio becoming the classifier's diagnostic input.
methods.stepDetail: Bring the two fitted slope labels together and retain their ratio as one boundary-type metric.
methods.transitionBridge: The observed ratio now enters each boundary class's empirical likelihood in M04.

methods.input: Each boundary-type slope-ratio dataset and candidate KDE bandwidths.
methods.output: One optimal bandwidth for each boundary-type dataset.
methods.validity: The source explicitly selects the bandwidth for each boundary-type dataset by cross-validation.
methods.locator: PDF pages 3-4, Section 2.4, paragraph following Equation (5)
methods.sourceExcerpt: In our application, cross-validation is used to select an optimal bandwidth for 3 F.-K. Huang et al. Journal of Hydrology 661 (2025) 133454 each boundary-type dataset.
methods.visualObject: Three boundary-type point clouds with bandwidth gauges settling at cross-validated values
methods.motionPurpose: Show bandwidth selection as the single operation that configures the class KDEs.
methods.stepDetail: Sweep each bandwidth gauge across its class dataset and retain the cross-validated optimum.
methods.transitionBridge: The likelihood curves next receive theory-informed class priors in M05.

methods.input: Ideal slope-ratio centers for recharge no-boundary and impervious conditions.
methods.output: Gaussian-like prior probability profiles centered near slope ratios zero one and two.
methods.validity: The profiles encode general knowledge of the theoretical slope ratios for the three boundary types.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.4, paragraph following Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: are constructed from Gaussian-like profiles, reflecting the general knowledge that these theoretical slope Parameter/Setting Initial value Search range ratios typically correspond to each boundary type.
methods.visualObject: three prior curves centered on ideal slope-ratio markers
methods.motionPurpose: Show the theoretical expectation supplied before observing a field ratio.
methods.stepDetail: Raise one prior profile around each ideal class center on the ratio axis.
methods.transitionBridge: Each class likelihood and prior become the paired inputs multiplied in M06.

methods.input: Each boundary class likelihood and its prior evaluated at the observed slope ratio.
methods.validity: The source explicitly forms one likelihood-times-prior value for each of the three boundary types before normalization.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.4, paragraph following Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: Once the ratio ������2∕������1 is determined using piecewise linear regres- Storage coefficient (������) 0.1 [1 × 10−6, 1] sion, we compute an unnormalized probability (likelihood × prior) Initial temperature (������0) 2000 N/A Cooling rate 0.85 N/A for each of the three boundary types.
methods.visualObject: Three likelihood-prior pairs multiplying into unnormalized class bars
methods.motionPurpose: Own the Bayesian product that creates the values consumed by posterior normalization.
methods.stepDetail: Pair each class likelihood with its prior; multiply each pair; retain three unnormalized bars.
methods.transitionBridge: The three unnormalized class values become the inputs rescaled to unit sum in M07.

methods.input: Three class likelihood-prior products evaluated at the observed slope ratio.
methods.output: Three posterior boundary probabilities whose sum equals one.
methods.validity: Normalization preserves the relative class weights and produces a probability distribution over the three classes.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.4, paragraph following Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: normalized so their sum is 1, yielding the posterior probability of each
methods.visualObject: three unnormalized bars rescaling to a unit-sum probability stack
methods.motionPurpose: Convert relative evidence into directly comparable posterior probabilities.
methods.stepDetail: Rescale the three class products until their displayed heights sum to one.
methods.transitionBridge: The posterior distribution now supports one boundary decision.

methods.input: Posterior probabilities for recharge no-boundary and impervious classes.
methods.output: One inferred hydrogeological boundary class for each drawdown record.
methods.validity: The decision follows the Bayesian posterior and the reported classifier thresholds for the observed slope ratio.
methods.sourceExcerpt: which is then used to classify the hydrological boundary
methods.visualObject: posterior probability stack resolving into one labeled boundary icon
methods.motionPurpose: Turn uncertainty-aware class evidence into the boundary type required by the image model.
methods.stepDetail: Highlight the supported class and carry its recharge or impervious sign forward.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the inferred class to choose the image-well sign.

methods.input: Impervious boundary class, pumping well, and candidate image well.
methods.output: Impervious-boundary image configuration with equal pumping rate.
methods.validity: For the impervious case, the source places the image well at equal distance across the boundary with an equal pumping rate.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2, impervious-boundary image construction preceding Equation (3)
methods.sourceExcerpt: for the case of impervious boundary, an image {recharge, no boundary, impervious}: well is placed at an equal distance on the opposite side of the boundary, with an equal pumping rate. This simulates the no-flow condition at the boundary.
methods.visualObject: Pumping well mirrored across the impervious boundary as an equal-rate image well
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the impervious class into its source-stated mirror-well configuration.
methods.stepDetail: Reflect the pumping well across the boundary at equal distance and retain the same pumping-rate sign.
methods.transitionBridge: Search image location and aquifer parameters against the observations.

methods.input: Candidate image-well location and aquifer parameters.
methods.output: Optimal image-well location and aquifer parameters.
methods.validity: The source applies simulated annealing to determine the optimum of the image-well and aquifer-parameter candidates.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.5, simulated-annealing application paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: The SA algorithm is applied to determine the optimal values of At a given temperature, the probability distribution of the sys- imaginary well location and aquifer parameters.
methods.visualObject: candidate image wells converging on a map while T and S meters stabilize
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the global search over geometry and hydraulic parameters.
methods.stepDetail: Move candidate image-well and aquifer-parameter markers through the search and retain the source-stated optimum.
methods.transitionBridge: Convert the optimized image point into a physical boundary line.

methods.input: Known pumping-well coordinates and optimized image-well coordinates.
methods.output: Linear boundary location and orientation angle theta.
methods.validity: The source develops a linear boundary equation and orientation expression from the known pumping-well and image-well coordinates.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Introduction, workflow paragraph preceding Section 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: With known coordinates of the image well and pumping more reliably. Vijai and Rohit (2011) presented a methodology for well, a linear equation is developed to delineate the boundary location, calculating average flux through boundary segments based on ground- and a mathematical expression is introduced to calculate its orientation.
methods.visualObject: well-to-image segment, midpoint, perpendicular boundary, and angle arc
methods.motionPurpose: Return an abstract optimization point to hydrogeological map geometry.
methods.stepDetail: Join the wells, mark the midpoint, rotate a line by 90 degrees, and display theta.
methods.transitionBridge: Check the complete workflow at recharge and impervious field sites.

methods.input: Boundary identification using two observation wells.
methods.output: Recommendation of at least three observation wells for reliable boundary identification.
methods.validity: The source states that two wells can mislocate the image well and recommends a minimum of three.
methods.locator: PDF page 10, Conclusions, observation-well requirement paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: the use of two observation wells may lead to incorrect location of the image well. Thus, a mini- Declaration of competing interest mum of three wells is suggested for reliable estimate in the boundary identification.
methods.visualObject: A failed two-well image location replaced by a three-well boundary-identification configuration
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the reported two-well failure into the source's minimum well-count recommendation.
methods.stepDetail: Offset the two-well image location as the reported failure; add a third observation well; retain the recommended configuration.
methods.transitionBridge: Return to the two field maps with the reliable minimum configuration.

symbols.meaning: semi-log slope ratio used to identify boundary type
symbols.transition: The two fitted lines collapse into one marker on the posterior curves.
The control stabilizes at the simulated-annealing optimum.
The control stabilizes with T and the image coordinates.
The point and pumping well define the boundary bisector.

symbols.meaning: orientation angle of the inferred linear boundary
symbols.transition: The arc anchors the inferred line to the field map.

return.visualObject: Tongue Valley and Dry Lake maps with pumping wells, image wells, three observation wells, and inferred recharge or impervious boundary lines
return.claim: The workflow identified the documented boundary type and jointly estimated image-well position, linear boundary geometry, T, and S at both field sites, with three-well results comparable to four-well results.
return.claimBoundary: The result requires at least three observation wells and the paper's confined, homogeneous, isotropic, straight-boundary model. Unconfined flow, leakage, heterogeneity, or non-Cooper-Jacob behavior requires site investigation before interpretation.
return.locator: PDF pages 7-9, Sections 4.1-4.3; PDF page 9, Conclusions
return.sourceExcerpt: wells are generally sufficient for determining image-well locations and

visual.primaryObject: pumping and observation wells near a straight hydrogeological boundary
visual.accentObject: signed image well and perpendicular-bisector boundary
visual.measuredQuantity: time-drawdown and semi-log slope ratio m_2/m_1
visual.geometryNotes: Use the actual field well layouts, mirror symmetry between pumping and image wells, and separate map treatments for the Tongue Valley recharge boundary and Dry Lake impervious boundary.
